skill.name: test-build-error
skill.description: Tests error visibility for build step failures
skill.summary: Tests error visibility for build step failures
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: The static weak cryptography finding at SKILL.md:4 is a false positive because that line is only metadata text. No prompt injection, credential access, or malicious intent was found, but the build step runs an unpinned npm install for a deliberately missing package name.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned npm Package Installation During Build
medium_findings.description: The build step runs an npm install for a package name that is expected not to exist. If that package name later becomes valid, build-time install scripts could introduce untrusted code execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The build command directly invokes npm install on an unpinned package name. The skill appears designed to fail, so this is a contextual supply-chain risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The static finding at SKILL.md:4 is dismissed. That line contains only the skill description, and no cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic usage appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is metadata text, not executable code or cryptographic configuration. The reviewed file contains no crypto API usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Build-Time Package Manager Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill executes npm during the build phase. Package manager execution can run remote package lifecycle scripts if the named package resolves.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is visible in the build configuration and would contact the npm registry. The named package is intentionally invalid, which lowers the immediate risk.

content.value_statement: Build systems can hide the real reason a skill fails before runtime. This skill creates a controlled build failure so reviewers can verify that errors remain visible.
actual_capabilities: Defines a Node 20 Alpine build environment.
Triggers a build failure through a missing npm package install.
Provides an empty input schema for a no-argument test run.
Documents the purpose as build error visibility testing.
Includes a runtime command that only matters if the build succeeds.
limitations: It is designed to fail during the build step.
It does not provide end-user automation after a failed build.
It depends on npm package resolution behavior.
It is useful mainly for marketplace and CI validation workflows.

use_cases.title: Validate Marketplace Build Logs
use_cases.description: Confirm that a marketplace pipeline shows package installation failures clearly during skill ingestion.

use_cases.title: Test CI Error Reporting
use_cases.description: Use a controlled failing skill to check whether CI jobs expose the correct build-stage failure.

use_cases.title: Review Skill Packaging Behavior
use_cases.description: Inspect how a skill package behaves when its build phase cannot complete.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Build Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Run this skill through the build pipeline and tell me whether the build failure is visible.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Build Log Cause
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the build output for this skill and summarize the exact dependency error in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Pipeline Error Surfaces
prompt_templates.prompt: Run this skill in two build environments and compare how each one reports the failed package install.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Build Failure Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether the skill ingestion pipeline preserves build-stage errors, exit status, and package manager context.

output_examples.input: Check whether the skill build failure is visible.
output_examples.output: The build failed during dependency installation. The report should identify the missing npm package and mark the failure as build-stage.

output_examples.input: Summarize the CI behavior for this skill.
output_examples.output: The CI system stopped before runtime. It showed the package installation failure, which confirms that build errors are not hidden.

output_examples.input: Assess whether this skill is ready for normal users.
output_examples.output: This skill is not meant for normal task execution. It is a diagnostic fixture for testing build failure reporting.

best_practices: Use it only in isolated marketplace or CI test environments.
Record the build logs before changing the dependency name.
Treat successful builds as a signal that the test fixture changed.
anti_patterns: Do not present it as a production automation skill.
Do not run it where outbound package installation is prohibited.
Do not replace targeted CI tests with this single failure case.

faq.question: What does this skill test?
faq.answer: It tests whether a build pipeline shows errors from a failed dependency installation.

faq.question: Is this skill expected to build successfully?
faq.answer: No. The build step is designed to fail by installing a package name that should not exist.

faq.question: Does it require user input?
faq.answer: No. The input schema is empty, so it does not require task parameters.

faq.question: Can it help debug CI systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It can show whether CI logs preserve the build-stage error and exit context.

faq.question: Is it safe for production workflows?
faq.answer: No. It is a diagnostic fixture and should not be used as production automation.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The build step invokes npm with an unpinned package name, which creates a supply-chain risk if that name later resolves.
